topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara’s hands were shaking so badly she had to grip the edge of the desk to keep them from trembling. The fluorescent lights of the library hummed, a grating soundtrack to the churning anxiety in her gut. She had spent the last three hours meticulously reviewing her personal statement, a masterpiece she felt was utterly inadequate. Then, tucked between two heavy textbooks, she found it: a perfectly polished, evocative essay, titled "The Unseen Architecture of Quiet." It was undeniably hers—the precise phrasing, the unexpected turn in the narrative—but it was published. A published example. A ghost of her own potential, laid bare for scrutiny. A sharp, cold feeling settled behind her ribs, tightening her chest until breathing became shallow and quick. She snatched the paper, her knuckles white, and immediately began frantically searching online, the frantic clicking of her mouse sounding deafening in the quiet study zone.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the screen, his jaw clenched so tightly a muscle twitched near his ear. The deadline was looming like a physical weight, pressing down on his shoulders until he felt he couldn't draw a full breath. He’d poured weeks into crafting an essay that felt authentic, a genuine window into who he was. But what he found wasn't his work; it was a flawless narrative, complete with a commendation from a prestigious institution. A wave of nausea washed over him. He reread the words, the sophisticated vocabulary echoing hollowly in his ears. He pushed his chair back with a scraping sound that seemed excessively loud. His eyes darted around the cramped dorm room, seeking an escape, anything to stop the relentless internal pressure building behind his eyes. He needed air, needed distance, needed to stop looking at the glowing text.

[story 3]

“No, no, this can’t be right,” whispered Chloe, her voice thin and reedy. She kept running a thumb over the printed page, the paper feeling slick and foreign under her touch. The sheer perfection of the prose was a physical assault; it felt like someone had stolen the very essence of her voice and perfected it into something untouchable. Her palms were damp against the glossy paper, and she kept blinking hard, trying to clear the sudden fog that had descended over her mind. Every instinct screamed at her to throw the document away, to erase the evidence of this sudden, crushing inadequacy. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between her bed and the window, her movements jerky and uncoordinated as the weight of expectation settled heavily upon her chest.
